title: How to Safely Approach an Injured or Fearful Dog
content:
1. Keep people safe first. Do not approach if traffic, fire, electrocution risk, or violence makes the scene unsafe.
2. Move slowly, turn your body slightly sideways, and avoid leaning over the dog.
3. Watch body language: hard stare, stiff posture, tucked tail, repeated lip licking, growling, snapping, or trying to crawl away all mean you should slow down or stop.
4. Do not corner the dog. Give it a clear exit route whenever possible.
5. Use a towel, blanket, board, or crate for transport only if this can be done without forcing the dog.
6. Even friendly dogs may bite when they are in pain. Keep your face away from the mouth.
7. If the dog is too distressed to handle safely, call a local rescue organization or veterinarian instead of forcing contact.

title: Bleeding Wounds and Punctures
content:
1. Apply firm, direct pressure with a clean cloth or towel.
2. Keep pressure in place for at least 3 minutes before checking.
3. If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top. Do not peel the first layer away.
4. Do not remove deeply embedded objects.
5. If you need to clean around a dirty wound, use saline or diluted povidone-iodine. Do not scrub inside a deep wound.
6. Deep punctures, heavy bleeding, wounds to the chest or abdomen, or wounds caused by another animal need veterinary care urgently because internal damage and infection are common.

title: Road Accidents, Fractures, and Possible Spinal Injury
content:
1. Make the area safe before you approach.
2. Assume pain, fracture, or internal injury until proven otherwise.
3. Do not pull on the legs or try to straighten bones.
4. Slide the dog onto a flat board, thick blanket, or sturdy cardboard so the body stays supported during transport.
5. Keep the dog warm and as still as possible.
6. Dogs that cannot stand, cry when touched, drag limbs, have obvious deformity, or seem weak or collapsed need urgent veterinary assessment.

title: Heatstroke and Dehydration
content:
Signs include heavy panting, drooling, weakness, vomiting, diarrhea, confusion, collapse, or gums that feel dry or sticky.

1. Move the dog into shade or a cool indoor space.
2. Start cooling right away with room-temperature water on the neck, armpits, belly, and groin.
3. Re-wet towels frequently or use a fan if available.
4. Do not use ice water or immerse the dog in very cold water.
5. Offer small amounts of water only if the dog is alert and able to swallow.
6. Heatstroke can turn fatal quickly, so veterinary care is urgent even if the dog seems better after initial cooling.

title: Possible Poisoning or Toxin Exposure
content:
Warning signs include sudden vomiting, drooling, tremors, seizures, bleeding, difficulty breathing, collapse, or sudden extreme weakness.

1. Remove the dog from the source if you can do it safely.
2. Keep the product, packet, plant sample, or vomit for identification.
3. Do not induce vomiting unless a veterinarian or poison control specialist tells you to.
4. Do not give milk, oil, salt, or home remedies.
5. Go to a veterinarian urgently and call a poison service where available.
6. Corrosive cleaners, rodenticides, pesticides, human medicines, chocolate, xylitol, grapes/raisins, and some plants are common dangerous exposures.

title: Maggot Wounds
content:
1. Maggot wounds need veterinary treatment as soon as possible.
2. Keep the dog safe, calm, and protected from more flies.
3. If you need to rinse the surface while arranging help, use clean saline. Do not pour kerosene, turpentine, engine oil, acid, or other chemicals into the wound.
4. Do not dig aggressively into the wound.
5. Cover loosely with a clean cloth or gauze for transport.
6. The dog may need thorough wound cleaning, pain control, antibiotics, and repeated follow-up care.

title: Mange, Skin Disease, and Severe Itching
content:
Patchy hair loss, thickened skin, redness, crusts, and relentless itching can be caused by mange, skin infection, flea allergy, fungal disease, or tick-borne illness.

1. Avoid home remedies like kerosene, engine oil, battery acid, chili, or turmeric paste.
2. If the skin is broken, painful, foul-smelling, or full of flies, arrange veterinary or rescue help promptly.
3. Wash your hands after handling because some causes of itching can temporarily irritate human skin.
4. If the problem is mild and the dog is handleable, focus first on nutrition, clean water, and a veterinary plan rather than random medicines.
5. Skin disease that involves the whole body, rapid weight loss, or weakness is not a minor problem.

title: Ticks and Tick-Borne Illness
content:
1. Heavy tick burdens can make dogs weak and anemic, and ticks can spread serious disease.
2. If you see pale gums, fever, bleeding, profound lethargy, or the dog stops eating, the dog needs veterinary care quickly.
3. You may remove visible ticks carefully with tweezers, pulling straight out close to the skin.
4. Do not crush ticks into wounds or apply harsh chemicals to raw skin.
5. A dog with many ticks plus weakness should not be treated as a simple grooming issue.

title: Eye Injuries and Eye Discharge
content:
1. If the eye is bulging, very cloudy, bleeding, or the dog cannot open it, seek veterinary care urgently.
2. Do not put steroid drops or random human eye drops into the eye.
3. If there is dirt around the eye, you can gently wipe the fur around it with saline or clean water.
4. Do not try to remove objects stuck in the eye.
5. Green or yellow discharge, swelling, or repeated squinting usually means the eye needs proper examination.

title: Choking and Breathing Distress
content:
1. If the dog is struggling to breathe, this is an emergency.
2. If the dog is still coughing and moving air, do not panic or put your hands deep into the mouth.
3. If you can clearly see an object near the front of the mouth and can remove it without pushing it deeper or getting bitten, do so carefully.
4. Blue or gray gums, silent distress, collapse, or inability to breathe means immediate veterinary help is needed.
5. Do not waste time on repeated attempts if the dog is worsening.

title: Seizures, Collapse, or Unresponsiveness
content:
1. Move objects away so the dog does not injure itself.
2. Do not put your hands near the mouth.
3. Time the event if you can.
4. Keep the dog cool, quiet, and away from stairs or traffic after the seizure.
5. Repeated seizures, seizures lasting more than a few minutes, collapse, or failure to wake up normally require urgent veterinary care.

title: Puppies, Orphans, and Newborn Litters
content:
1. Do not move a litter immediately unless the puppies are in direct danger.
2. Watch from a distance first because the mother may be nearby and returning.
3. Warmth matters more than feeding in the first moments for cold, weak newborns.
4. Very young puppies can aspirate easily if fed incorrectly, so bottle-feeding plans should come from an experienced rescuer or veterinarian.
5. Crying constantly, cold bodies, injuries, fly strike, or a missing mother for many hours are reasons to escalate for help quickly.

title: Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Refusing Food
content:
1. Repeated vomiting, bloody diarrhea, black stool, or a swollen painful belly are urgent red flags.
2. Puppies dehydrate very quickly and can crash fast.
3. If the dog is alert and not vomiting constantly, offer water in small amounts.
4. Do not give human anti-diarrheal or pain medicines.
5. If the dog cannot keep water down, seems weak, or stops responding normally, seek veterinary care.

title: Dog Bites, Scratches, and Rabies Exposure for People
content:
For people, rabies risk must be taken seriously.

1. Wash any bite, scratch, or saliva exposure on broken skin with soap and running water for at least 15 minutes.
2. Seek medical care immediately for rabies post-exposure assessment.
3. Do not wait for symptoms.
4. Report the animal through local animal welfare or public health channels where appropriate.
5. Human safety comes first; if a rescue scene is unsafe, call emergency services.

title: Animal Cruelty, Complaints, and ABC Rules in India
content:
1. If you witness cruelty, document what you safely can: time, location, photos, video, and witness details.
2. The Government of India announced the Animal Birth Control Rules, 2023 under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960.
3. According to the April 18, 2023 PIB release, relocation of dogs cannot be permitted, and local bodies are responsible for sterilization and anti-rabies work under the rules.
4. AWBI can be contacted for grievances and escalation, but it is not a field ambulance dispatch team.
5. If a person is in immediate danger, use public emergency services first.

title: Finding Help in India and Beyond
content:
India-wide verified starting points:
- Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) contact: 0129-2555700.
- AWBI grievance line: 09650609880 and the online grievance portal.
- AWBI also publishes a directory of recognized animal welfare organizations that can help you find a local organization in cities not listed below.

Selected regional resources with clear published scope:
- Mumbai: BSPCA hospital enquiry +91 85916 59398; their hospital says it serves emergency cases 24x7.
- Delhi NCR: Friendicoes SECA helpline and ambulance service, with a published helpline window of 9am-9pm; Friendicoes also says its night clinic and night ambulance are currently suspended.
- Delhi / guidance: Jaagruti offers guidance and first-aid advice and states on its site that it is not a rescue service, hospital, or ambulance.
- Pune: RESQ office phone +91 98909 99111. RESQ says its 24x7 helplines are for wildlife emergencies and large domestic animals, with technical rescues for all animals depending on the case.
- Udaipur: Animal Aid Unlimited asks callers to use 9829843726 / 9602325253 / 9784005989 for injured street animals in Udaipur and to stay with the animal if possible.
- Greater Chennai: Blue Cross of India uses an online rescue request form for distressed street animals within its service area and says street-animal ABC requests within corporation limits should go to Corporation Helpline 1913.

Global fallback guidance:
- Search for the nearest open emergency veterinarian in maps.
- For poison exposure, call a local emergency veterinarian or a poison service where available.
- If a person may have rabies exposure or the scene is unsafe, prioritize human emergency care immediately.
